content:
Target codebase (hourly) has 45+ oRPC contracts, 100+ Prisma models, 50+ React pages, existing Playwright + playwright-bdd setup with auth/login.feature (13 step defs). Rich dataset for MVP validation." 2>&1 && \
DECISION: Match algorithm is tiered (Cucumber expressions → FTS5 fuzzy → LLM-assisted creation) and serves as a GROWTH mechanism. When no Action matches, the algorithm packages graph context as a suggestion for the agent to create a new Action. The graph learns from every feature." 2>&1 && \
DECISION: Scanner architecture is pluggable from day 1. Scanner interface with registry, concrete implementations for oRPC, Prisma, React Router. Extensible to Express, GraphQL, etc." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: MCP server uses @modelcontextprotocol/server with stdio transport, Zod v4 for input schemas, better-sqlite3 sync API for instant responses. All logging to stderr." 2>&1 && \
FACT: CLI uses Commander.js + @commander-js/extra-typings for type-safe args. Binary names: abacus, ab. All commands support --json flag." 2>&1

Switched from TypeScript to Go for core. Reasons: better compiler feedback for agents, single binary distribution, matches beads ecosystem (also Go/cobra), no CGo needed (modernc.org/sqlite). Scanners remain Node.js (BYOASTP)." 2>&1 && \
BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) architecture. Scanners are standalone executables communicating via Scanner Protocol v1 (JSON over stdin/stdout). Abacus core has zero language-specific parsing. Any language can write a scanner." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: Go MCP SDK uses mcp.AddTool() with struct-based input schemas (auto-generated via jsonschema tags). StdioTransport for Claude Code. MCP server is a cobra subcommand in the same binary: abacus mcp-server." 2>&1 && \
FACT: Official Go libraries exist for all needed components: MCP SDK (v1.4.0), cucumber-expressions-go, gherkin/go/v38, modernc.org/sqlite. Zero third-party gaps." 2>&1

TypeScript compiler messages are poor for agent feedback loops. When Sonnet agents hit TS compiler errors, messages are often cryptic ('Type X is not assignable to Y' with deeply nested generics) and lead to thrashing. Go compiler messages are precise, actionable, and include suggestions ('did you mean X?'). For agent-built tools, compiler feedback quality is a first-class design concern — choose languages where agents can self-correct from error output." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) — when a tool needs to parse multiple languages/frameworks, don't embed parsers. Instead, define a versioned JSON protocol over stdin/stdout and spawn parsers as subprocesses. Benefits: (1) core stays language-agnostic, (2) parsers can be written in whatever language parses that stack best, (3) fault isolation — crashing parser can't take down the host, (4) independent versioning, (5) community extensibility without forking. The protocol IS the interface. Trade-off: subprocess startup overhead (~100-500ms), but acceptable for scan-frequency operations." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: Match failures as growth signals. When designing a lookup/match system, make the 'not found' path productive. Instead of returning empty, package the search context (what was queried, what related items exist) as a structured creation suggestion. The caller (agent or human) reviews and persists. This transforms every failure into a learning event. Applied in Abacus: Gherkin step with no matching Action → system returns related Routes/Entities/Pages + pre-filled Action template → agent creates Action → future queries succeed. The system gets smarter with each miss." 2>&1 && \
FACT: Go has a complete native ecosystem for building MCP-enabled CLI tools with graph storage: (1) github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0 — official MCP SDK with mcp.AddTool() and StdioTransport, (2) github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go — pattern matching with parameter extraction, (3) github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go/v38 — Gherkin file pa

content:
bd update aba-zca.16 -d "$(cat <<'DESC
## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions �
bead:
bd update aba-zca.16 -d "$(cat <<'DESC'
## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions — they reveal:
- Auth requirements (requireAuth, requireRole)
- Validation (validateBody, validateParams)
- Rate limiting (rateLimiter)
- File handling (upload.single)

Store as array in properties: \`"middleware": ["requireAuth", "requireRole", "validateBody"]\`

### Relationship to oRPC Scanner
When both scanners run, Abacus will have two Route nodes for the same endpoint — one from oRPC (contract) and one from Express (runtime). The CLI scan command should detect these overlaps and create \`relates_to\` edges between them. This is handled by the SyncScanResults logic in the Go core, not by this scanner.

## Testing

- [ ] Parses router.get/post/put/delete → correct Route nodes
- [ ] Handles parameterized routes (:id, :slug)
- [ ] Extracts middleware chain names
- [ ] Detects app.use() prefix mounting and composes full paths
- [ ] Handles router.route() chaining
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Express route files

## Validation

- [ ] All 30+ hourly Express route files scanned
- [ ] Middleware chains captured (reveals auth/validation requirements)
- [ ] Route mounting prefixes correctly composed
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/express/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/express/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/express/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
DESC
)" 2>&1 && \
aba-zca

content: Go module cache at /Users/mjn/go/pkg is read-only in the Claude Code sandbox. Must use GOMODCACHE=/tmp/.claude_scratch/gomodcache GONOSUMDB=* GONOSUMCHECK=* for all go commands.
Preserved existing .gitignore entries (.dolt/, *.db) from beads init and appended new Go-specific entries.
modernc.org/sqlite supports FTS5 and unixepoch() out of the box — no special build tags needed. GOMODCACHE must be an absolute path.
Used content-sync FTS5 (content='nodes', content_rowid='rowid') with triggers rather than external-content manual sync, keeping FTS automatically in sync with the nodes table.

Pure-Go SQLite setup with modernc.org/sqlite: driver name is 'sqlite' (NOT 'sqlite3' which is mattn's CGO driver). Pragmas must be individual Exec() calls, not batched. Set WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, foreign_keys=ON, cache_size=-64000 (64MB), busy_timeout=5000. If any pragma fails, Close() the db before returning to prevent leaked connections." 2>/dev/null

FTS5 content-sync trigger pattern for SQLite. The delete command syntax is counterintuitive: INSERT INTO table_fts(table_fts, rowid, cols) VALUES ('delete', old.rowid, old.cols). Table name appears as both target and column. UPDATE triggers must delete-then-insert (FTS5 has no native update). content_rowid='rowid' ties to SQLite's implicit rowid — breaks if table uses WITHOUT ROWID." 2>/dev/null

SQLite graph adjacency list with enforced types. CHECK constraints on kind columns enforce enums at DB level. UNIQUE(src_id, dst_id, kind) prevents duplicate edges of same kind but allows multiple edge types between same nodes. ON DELETE CASCADE on edges auto-cleans when nodes are deleted. Properties as JSON TEXT DEFAULT '{}' — extensible without migrations, queryable via json_extract(). Adding new kinds requires schema migration (intentional — schema is the contract)." 2>/dev/null

Cobra CLI with ldflags version injection. Declare 'var Version = \"dev\"' (must be var, not const — linker can't override constants). Build with '-ldflags \"-X full/package/path.Version=$(VERSION)\"'. Full import path required — wrong path silently does nothing. Cobra's rootCmd.Version field + SetVersionTemplate gives --version flag for free. VERSION ?= $(shell git describe --tags --always --dirty) with fallback to 'dev'." 2>/dev/null
Used a --dir flag on init command to allow testing in temp directories. This keeps tests isolated without needing to chdir.
cobra rootCmd is a package-level var, so tests reuse it across test functions. The --dir flag on init avoids test pollution by pointing at t.TempDir().

ValidateOutput returns []string (error messages) rather than error, making it composable — callers can check len(errs) == 0 and format/join as needed. The runner joins them with semicolons for its error return.

FTS5 content-sync join requires joining on rowid (n.rowid = fts.rowid), not on id. The content='nodes' and content_rowid='rowid' config in the FTS5 virtual table means FTS stores rowid references, not the text id primary key.
Used sql.NullString for nullable fields (source_file, scan_hash) to handle NULL values cleanly in scan operations, converting to *string pointers on the GraphNode struct.

Recursive CTE with UNION (not UNION ALL) for cycle-safe bidirectional graph traversal in SQLite. UNION inherently deduplicates visited nodes, preventing infinite loops in cyclic graphs without maintaining an explicit visited set. Query shape: WITH RECURSIVE connected(id, depth) AS (VALUES(?,0) UNION SELECT e.dst_id, c.depth+1 FROM edges e JOIN connected c ON e.src_id=c.id WHERE c.depth<? UNION SELECT e.src_id, c.depth+1 FROM edges e JOIN connected c ON e.dst_id=c.id WHERE c.depth<?). Two UNION branches traverse both edge directions." 2>/dev/null

validNodeKinds and validEdgeKinds are duplicated across db/types.go (Go constants) and scanner/validate.go (map[string]bool allowlists). Adding a new node or edge kind requires updating BOTH locations plus the SQLite CHECK constraint in schema.go. These are NOT derived from a single source of truth. This is the primary extensibility gotcha — forgetting one location causes silent validation failures." 2>/dev/null

RunAll merges scanner outputs by concatenation but does NOT run cross-scanner validation. Edges from scanner A referencing nodes from scanner B pass per-scanner validation but could fail at DB insert time. Also, two scanners emitting the same node ID results in last-write-wins via BulkUpsertNodes. No dedup detection exists at the merge layer. This is acceptable for MVP but needs SyncScanResults logic to handle properly." 2>/dev/null

Go repository testing with real SQLite via t.TempDir() — no mocking. setupTestDB creates a real in-memory or temp-file SQLite DB with full schema, registered for cleanup via t.Cleanup. This ensures DB behavior (triggers, constraints, FTS5) is tested faithfully. Combined with builder helpers (makeNode, validOutput) that provide default-valid test data where tests modify one field to trigger specific errors." 2>/dev/null

Subprocess plugin system with fail-open aggregation. RunAll captures per-scanner errors into MergedScanOutput.Errors and continues executing remaining scanners. Callers must check len(merged.Errors) > 0 after RunAll — a nil error return does NOT mean all scanners succeeded. This is the standard pattern for batch operations where partial results are more valuable than all-or-nothing." 2>/dev/null

After JSON round-trip through SQLite (MarshalProperties/UnmarshalProperties), Go []string stored in properties becomes []interface{} when read back. Tests must use []any type assertion, not []string." 2>&1

ActionService.deleteEdgesFromByKind accesses repo.database directly (unexported field, same package) rather than adding a new method to GraphRepository. This keeps the repository API unchanged per file ownership rules." 2>&1
Used Tokenize() as shared function between FuzzyMatcher and SuggestionBuilder to avoid duplication. Exported it for testability.

React Router scanner parses JSX <Route> elements with ts-morph (needs jsx: react-jsx in tsconfig). Handles nesting via parent walk for path composition, index routes, catch-all, and protection detection via ternary+Navigate pattern. 14 tests." 2>/dev/null

Scanner Template — all 4 scanners share identical skeleton: (1) index.ts reads ScanInput from stdin, calls parser, writes ScanOutput to stdout; (2) parser.ts has async function returning ScanOutput with try/catch per file → warnings accumulation; (3) types.ts duplicates protocol types (should be shared @abacus/scanner-protocol package); (4) Deterministic IDs: route:{METHOD}-{path}, entity:{ModelName}, page:{fullPath}; (5) Stats tracking: filesScanned, nodesFound, edgesFound, errors, durationMs. Template is identical enough to generate new scanners from." 2>/dev/null

Regex vs ts-morph for scanners — use regex for non-JS/TS inputs (Prisma schemas, YAML, SQL, .env) and ts-morph for actual TypeScript/JavaScript/JSX source code. The decision boundary is clear: if the input has a TS parser, use it. ts-morph requires skipAddingFilesFromTsConfig:true and noEmit:true to stay fast (parse-only, no type resolution). JSX parsing needs jsx:4 (ReactJSX) in compiler options." 2>/dev/null

AST chain walking for fluent APIs — when parsing builder patterns like oc.route({}).input(S).output(S) or router.route('/path').get(h).put(h), the innermost call (.route()) is the deepest AST node. Walk UP via Parent→PropertyAccessExpression→Parent→CallExpression to find chained methods. Use a bounded loop (5-20 iterations) or walk until parent===null. Both oRPC and Express scanners use this technique." 2>/dev/null

Scanner development checklist — (1) Always emit valid ScanOutput even on fatal errors (Prisma index.ts is the gold standard); (2) Use try/catch per file with warning accumulation, never let one bad file kill the scan; (3) Generate deterministic IDs from content ({kind}:{unique_key}); (4) Set source:'scan' and sourceFile (relative) on all nodes; (5) Return edges:[] and warnings:[] (not undefined) when empty; (6) Only emit edges for relationships your scanner can structurally detect (cross-scanner edges belong in a stitching phase); (7) Test protocol conformance by looping over all nodes asserting required fields." 2>/dev/null

Scanner code duplication opportunities — (1) resolveGlobs() is copy-pasted between oRPC and Express; (2) ScanOutput types are duplicated 4x across types.ts files; (3) stdin reading has two inconsistent patterns (sync readFileSync vs async chunked); (4) per-file try/catch→warning loop is identical in all ts-morph scanners. These should become a shared @abacus/scanner-protocol package. Prisma scanner is the cleanest reference (separates parsing from output building, most robust error handling, most complete test coverage)." 2>/dev/null

React Router JSX scanner nuances — (1) Must handle both JsxSelfClosingElement (<Route .../>) and JsxElement (<Route>children</Route>) with different APIs; (2) Path composition for nested routes requires recursive parent walk (getParentRoutePath); (3) Protected route detection uses heuristic: walk up AST for ConditionalExpression inside JsxExpression where falsy branch contains Navigate; (4) Component extraction unwraps: JsxAttribute→JsxExpression→inner JsxSelfClosingElement→tag name. Most complex of the 4 scanners due to nesting and contextual detection." 2>/dev/null

CLI query commands reuse the same GraphRepository/ActionService methods as the MCP server handlers. The CLI is a thin output-formatting layer over the same query API. Shared queryNodesCmd helper handles routes/entities/pages identically." 2>/dev/null

Shared queryNodesCmd() factory function creates cobra commands for routes/entities/pages. Each specific command file (routes.go, entities.go, pages.go) is ~10 lines calling this factory with NodeKind. Extra columns per kind: routes→METHOD, entities→FIELDS count, pages→PATH." 2>/dev/null

Cobra persistent BoolVar flags leak between tests in the same package. Once --json is set true by one test, it stays true for subsequent tests using the same rootCmd. Fix: explicitly set --json=false in tests that expect human-readable output." 2>/dev/null

CLI package now has 33 tests total: 9 init, 8 match/coverage, 16 scan/query. All commands follow the pattern: open DB → create repo/services → query → format output (JSON or table). Shared queryNodesCmd factory generates 3 commands from a single function." 2>/dev/null

Cobra command factory for kind-filtered queries. When multiple CLI commands differ only by a filter parameter (NodeKind), create a factory function queryNodesCmd(kind, use, short) that returns a pre-configured *cobra.Command. Each concrete command file is ~10 lines calling the factory. Extra columns per kind are handled via a switch inside the factory. Applied: routes.go, entities.go, pages.go all use queryNodesCmd from nodes.go. Reduces N*120 lines to N*10 + 120." 2>/dev/null

CLI-as-thin-wrapper-over-services. CLI commands and MCP server handlers call the same underlying services (GraphRepository, ActionService, MatchService, scanner.Runner) — they differ only in output formatting (table vs JSON-RPC). This ensures behavioral parity: if a query works in MCP, it works in CLI. Pattern: open DB → create repo/services → call same method → format for output target." 2>/dev/null

Cobra persistent BoolVar flags leak between test invocations on the same rootCmd. Once --json is set true by one test's SetArgs+Execute, the bound variable stays true for subsequent tests. Cobra does NOT reset persistent flags to defaults between Execute() calls. Fix: explicitly pass --json=false in tests expecting human-readable output. This affects any persistent flag bound via BoolVar/StringVar." 2>/dev/null

Scan pipeline — config → runner → node ingest → edge ingest. The scan command: (1) resolves config.yaml path from --db flag path, (2) filters scanners by optional type arg, (3) runs scanner.Runner.RunAll() which spawns subprocesses, (4) converts ScanNode→GraphNode and bulk upserts, (5) ingests edges one-by-one with warn-on-failure (edges may reference cross-scanner nodes not yet ingested). Same pipeline in MCP scanHandler but with different output formatting." 2>/dev/null

Abacus CLI now has 8 commands beyond init/version: scan, routes, entities, pages, actions (with create subcommand), graph, stats, match, coverage. All support --json flag. 33 CLI tests total across 4 test files. The actions command uses parent+subcommand pattern (actionsCmd.AddCommand(actionsCreateCmd)) for extensibility (future: actions delete, actions update)." 2>/dev/null

Use mcp.NewInMemoryTransports() from Go MCP SDK for in-process MCP testing. Returns paired (serverTransport, clientTransport) via net.Pipe(). Server_test.go already tests handlers directly — the E2E test layers on transport to prove full JSON-RPC protocol. See .gomodcache/github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk@v1.4.0/mcp/client_example_test.go for client usage." 2>&1

Existing test helpers: setupTestDB (cli/match_test.go:15-48), setupTestDBWithNodes (cli/query_test.go:17-77), openTestDB/openAndInitTestDB (db/db_test.go:10-28), setupTestServer (mcp/server_test.go:15-42). Echo scanner fixture at testdata/scanners/echo-scanner.sh." 2>&1

Skills will be delivered as a Claude Code plugin inside the abacus repo. Structure: plugin.json + skills/abacus-validate/SKILL.md + commands/. Anyone who clones abacus and installs the plugin gets the skills. Reference: ~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/example-plugin/ for plugin structure." 2>&1
Scanner config format in config.yaml uses map[string]ScannerConfig where keys are scanner IDs, not array entries. Skills reference the CLI commands directly via Bash tool.

detectProject monorepo support — must scan subdirectory package.json files (backend/, frontend/) and Prisma paths (backend/prisma/schema.prisma). Root package.json in monorepos often only has client deps. oRPC detection broadened to any @orpc/* prefix." 2>/dev/null

abacus init --force + scanner auto-detection required 3 iterations to work against real project (hourly). Issues: (1) old binary on PATH, (2) no --force flag to re-detect, (3) monorepo structure with deps split across subdirectory package.json files and Prisma in backend/prisma/. Each was only discoverable by testing against a real project, not unit tests." 2>/dev/null

Three scanners generated duplicate node/edge IDs because ID templates lacked sufficient discriminating context. Express/oRPC used route:\${METHOD}-\${path} which collided when multiple router files defined same relative paths (GET /, POST /, GET /:id). Prisma used edge:entity:\${model}-field_relation-entity:\${target} which collided when a model had multiple FKs to the same target entity. Root cause: ID generation assumed 1:1 mapping between ID components and real entities, but real-world projects have many-files-to-same-paths and many-fields-to-same-target patterns." 2>/dev/null

Scanner ID uniqueness rule — every node/edge ID must encode ALL dimensions necessary for global uniqueness: (1) entity kind, (2) source file path (normalized), (3) local discriminator. Mental test: if someone copy-pasted the source file to a second location, would IDs collide? If yes, missing a dimension. Express/oRPC fix: route:\${METHOD}-\${routerName}:\${path} where routerName derived from relPath via deriveRouterName(). Prisma fix: edge:entity:\${model}-field_relation:\${fieldName}-entity:\${target}." 2>/dev/null

deriveRouterName/deriveContractName helper pattern — strips common prefixes (src/, backend/, routes/, routers/, api/), file extensions (.routes.ts, .router.ts, .ts, .js), and trailing /index from relPath to produce a stable human-readable namespace. E.g. backend/routes/users.routes.ts → users, src/contracts/auth/login.ts → auth/login. Fallback returns original relPath if stripping produces empty string." 2>/dev/null

RunAll merges scanner outputs by concatenation with NO cross-scanner dedup detection. Two scanners emitting the same node ID results in last-write-wins via BulkUpsertNodes. This means ID uniqueness must be enforced at the scanner level, not the merge layer. Future improvement: add a runtime Set<string> collision detector in the ingestion pipeline as a safety net." 2>/dev/null

Chose to derive router/contract names from file paths rather than from code-level identifiers (variable names like 'usersRouter'). Rationale: file paths are always available and stable, while variable names require additional AST analysis, may be inconsistent, and don't work for default exports. The deriveXxxName() approach is O(1) string manipulation vs O(n) AST traversal." 2>/dev/null

Made ignorePaths project-level (project.ignorePaths in config.yaml) rather than per-scanner. Rationale: most ignores (node_modules, dist, tests) apply to all scanners. Per-scanner already has inclusion globs (routeGlobs, contractGlobs). Project-level avoids redundant config. Passed via ScanInput JSON so scanners receive it automatically." 2>/dev/null

ignorePaths matching uses prefix comparison on relative paths: m === ip || m.startsWith(ip + '/'). This matches both exact directory names (dist) and path prefixes (src/demo). All 4 TS scanners use identical predicate. Go side passes the array through ScanInput.IgnorePaths. Default ignores set during abacus init: node_modules, dist, build, .git." 2>/dev/null

Live progress in CLI scan commands — instead of using RunAll() which batches everything, iterate scanners individually with RunScanner() and print progress to stderr between calls. Format: 'Scanning with <id>... done (N nodes, N edges, Nms)'. Progress goes to os.Stderr so --json stdout is unaffected. Gate with showProgress = !quiet && !jsonFlag." 2>/dev/null

Five-layer duplicate prevention for graph scanners — (1) File-path prefix in node IDs for cross-file uniqueness (route:GET-users:/), (2) Field name in edge IDs for multi-FK disambiguation (field_relation:fieldName), (3) DB constraint relaxation (drop UNIQUE(src,dst,kind), keep id PK), (4) Within-file Set+counter dedup for same-path routes in one file (#2 suffix), (5) ignorePaths config for directory-level exclusion. Each layer catches a different class of duplicate. No single fix is sufficient for real-world projects." 2>/dev/null

CLI query commands (routes, entities, pages) had a hardcoded default limit of 50, causing truncated output that looked like incomplete data. Changed default to 1000 and flag help to '0 = all'. The limit flag is defined in queryNodesCmd factory in nodes.go." 2>/dev/null

README structure follows 8 sections matching bead spec: How It Works (ASCII diagram), Quick Start, CLI Commands (all 12), MCP Server (11 tools table), Scanner Protocol V1 (full JSON schemas), Writing a New Scanner, Built-in Scanners, Project Structure. Kept professional and scannable with tables for flags/tools/kinds." 2>/dev/null

MCP server uses newline-delimited JSON-RPC (not Content-Length framing) via gomcp StdioTransport. 11 tools registered: query_routes, query_entities, query_pages, query_actions, create_action, update_action, match_step, match_scenario, graph_context, scan, stats.

FTS5 MATCH queries crash with 'syntax error near /' when user text contains special chars like / * + - ^ ~. The Tokenize() function in match/fuzzy.go must strip ALL non-alphanumeric characters, not just common punctuation. Fix: iterate bytes keeping only a-z 0-9, instead of strings.Trim with a limited charset. Discovered via 'abacus match -f login.feature' where step 'I navigate to /dashboard' broke FTS5.

ActionService.List() returned 0 for routes/entities/pages because it only fetched nodes without resolving edge references, unlike Get() which resolved them. Fix: added edge resolution loop to List() matching Get()'s pattern. General rule: list views displaying relationship counts must query edges, not just nodes.

Go filepath.Glob does NOT support ** for recursive directory matching — it silently returns no matches. Must use filepath.WalkDir with manual pattern matching for recursive globbing. Affected abacus coverage command's default pattern **/*.feature. Fix: implemented globFeatureFiles() that splits on ** and walks with filepath.Match on the suffix.

When building FTS5 queries from user-provided text (Gherkin steps, search terms), always sanitize tokens to alphanumeric-only before constructing the MATCH clause. FTS5 operators (AND OR NOT NEAR + - * ^) and path separators (/) cause syntax errors if passed through. Tokenize once, validate chars, then join with OR.
When building FTS5 queries from user text, always sanitize tokens to alphanumeric-only before MATCH. FTS5 operators (AND OR NOT NEAR + - * ^) and path separators (/) cause syntax errors.

/abacus-validate skill runs 12-step pipeline validation. All read operations pass. DB writes blocked by Claude Code sandbox when target project is outside the repo — this is expected. JSON key casing is inconsistent: graph command uses uppercase (Nodes, Edges) from Go struct marshaling, stats uses lowercase. Cosmetic issue.

Added json struct tags to all 11 Go structs that get serialized via PrintJSON. Keys now consistently use snake_case (nodes, edges, action, tier, gherkin_patterns, route_refs, etc.). Previously Go default marshaling used PascalCase (Nodes, Edges) for structs without tags, while leaf types (GraphNode, GraphEdge) already had lowercase tags — causing inconsistent JSON APIs.

DECISION: Use LSP (ts-morph for TS) to trace real import chains rather than heuristic name matching. Zero config — LSP already knows the project graph from tsconfig.json.
DECISION: Extend existing scanner protocol with phase: link rather than creating a separate linker protocol. Minimizes protocol surface area.
DECISION: Language-agnostic linker protocol — TS linker is just the first implementation. Anyone can build a linker using their language's LSP (pyright, gopls, Eclipse JDT, etc.).
Only Prisma scanner currently creates edges (field_relation between entities). Express, oRPC, and React Router produce node-only subgraphs. Architecture already supports cross-scanner edges — node IDs are globally unique, DB accepts any src/dst.
PATTERN: Linker scanner follows the compiler/linker analogy — scanners are compilers producing object files (subgraphs), linker resolves cross-references. Keeps scanners simple and independent.

FACT: ValidateOutput at scanner/validate.go:70-76 checks that every edge srcId/dstId appears in the same outputs nodes array. Linker has zero nodes and many cross-scanner edges. This MUST be fixed or linker output is always rejected.
FACT: Go map iteration order is random. cfg.Scanners is map[string]ScannerConfig. Must explicitly filter by phase in two passes, not rely on iteration order.
FACT: CLI scan.go uses RunScanner individually (not RunAll) for progress reporting. Refactor the scan loop, not RunAll.
oRPC routes lack handler metadata — contractFile and schema are stored but not the implementation file. Linker must search for files that import the contract and call .handler()/.func() to find implementations.
PATTERN: Prisma client property access is prisma.modelName.method() (lowercase). Entity nodes are entity:ModelName (PascalCase). Need case-insensitive entityByName map.

Plan review found: (1) need edge provenance tracking (source_scanner on edges) for correct stale cleanup, (2) ExistingNodes should be scoped to route+entity with stripped Properties for security/perf, (3) file-level entity-reference cache is critical perf lever, (4) trace depth should be capped at 6-8 not unlimited

Applied plan review feedback. 10 recommendations applied across 3 beads (aba-iu1.1, aba-iu1.2, aba-iu1.5). 2 beads merged (aba-iu1.3→aba-iu1.2, aba-iu1.4→aba-iu1.5). Key changes: (1) added edge provenance via source_scanner column, (2) scoped ExistingNodes to ScanNodeRef (no Properties), (3) added file-level entity-reference cache + capped trace depth at 8.

When adding post-scan processing phases to the scanner protocol: (1) Add Phase field to ScannerConfig (string, defaults to scan for backward compat), (2) Split runner execution into explicit phase-filtered passes (dont rely on map iteration order in Go), (3) Pass results from earlier phases via ScanInput.ExistingNodes, (4) Use lightweight reference types (ScanNodeRef: id/kind/name/sourceFile only) not full node payloads to limit blast radius and payload size, (5) Modify ValidateOutput to accept knownNodeIDs for cross-scanner edge validation, (6) Add source_scanner column to edges table for provenance-based stale cleanup. This pattern generalizes beyond link phase to any future phases (enrich, validate, etc.).

When passing graph data to scanner subprocesses, use a lightweight reference type (ScanNodeRef: id, kind, name, sourceFile) instead of full node payloads with Properties maps. Reasons: (1) Security — Properties can contain PII-adjacent data like field definitions, middleware names, route params that leak to subprocess, (2) Performance — Prisma entities with 30+ fields produce KB per node, multiplied by hundreds of nodes = multi-MB payloads, (3) The linker only needs identity and location info to start tracing, not the full metadata. If full properties are needed later, the linker can request them via a DB query or enriched protocol.

When tracing import chains through TypeScript files to find entity references, maintain a file-level result cache: Map<filePath, Set<entityName>>. ts-morph caches parsed ASTs internally (no need for custom AST caching), but the entity-reference ANALYSIS per file is the real repeated cost. Without this cache, barrel files (index.ts re-exporting 50+ services) cause O(routes x barrel_fanout) redundant AST walks. With the cache, repeated visits are O(1) lookup. This is the single biggest performance lever for the linker — difference between 15-20s and 60-90s for 200 routes.

Scanner protocol phase extension — add Phase to config, split runner into explicit phase-filtered passes, use lightweight ScanNodeRef for cross-process data, add source_scanner to edges for provenance. Generalizes to any future phase (enrich, validate).
File-level entity-reference cache (Map<filePath, Set<entityName>>) is the #1 perf lever — ts-morph caches ASTs natively but analysis results must be cached manually. Without it: O(routes × barrel_fanout). With it: O(1) per repeated visit.
Edge provenance uses nullable source_scanner TEXT column with index. InsertEdge passes it through as *string. Null means legacy/unknown scanner origin.
Review found 46 findings across 6 agents, deduped to 7 actionable items. Most P1s were by-design multi-bead scaffolding — context about epic structure is critical for accurate review triage.

BulkUpsertEdges uses FK-tolerant transaction pattern — checks strings.Contains(err, 'FOREIGN KEY constraint failed') per-row and continues. This gives best-effort semantics for cross-scanner edges where referenced nodes may not exist yet.

Two-pass CLI scan flow — DB opens between phases. Scan-phase runs first (60s timeout), nodes ingested, then link-phase (5min timeout) receives ExistingNodes. When user filters to a link-phase scanner, existing nodes come from DB query instead of scan-phase results. Stale edges deleted by source_scanner before BulkUpsertEdges.

Two-pass scanner execution requires DB to open between phases. Scan-phase runs all phase=scan scanners (60s timeout), ingests nodes+edges, then link-phase runs phase=link scanners (5min timeout) with ExistingNodes populated. When user filters to a standalone link-phase scanner (e.g. 'abacus scan linker'), existing nodes must come from DB query (GetNodeRefsByKinds) since there are no scan-phase results in that run.

FK-tolerant bulk transactions in Go/SQLite — when bulk-inserting edges that may reference nodes from other scanners, check each stmt.Exec error for 'FOREIGN KEY constraint failed', log a warning, and continue. Commit the transaction with all successful rows. This gives best-effort semantics where partial results are more valuable than all-or-nothing. Pattern: tx.Begin → stmt.Prepare → loop(Exec, check FK, continue) → tx.Commit.

Caller-controlled phase orchestration — the Runner stays a dumb subprocess executor (RunScanner runs one command). The CLI scan.go owns orchestration: separating scanners by phase, controlling execution order, managing data flow between phases (ScanNodeRef collection), and handling different timeout/ingestion strategies per phase. This is better than baking phase awareness into Runner because: (1) Runner stays simple and testable, (2) MCP server can implement its own phase orchestration later, (3) separation of concerns.

MCP server (mcp/server.go) uses RunAll which only runs scan-phase scanners. It does NOT support link-phase execution yet. When adding link-phase support to MCP, the scanHandler needs the same two-pass logic as CLI scan.go: filter by phase, ingest scan nodes first, then run linkers with ExistingNodes. This is a future task, not in scope for aba-iu1.2.

Stale edge cleanup is scoped by source_scanner column. DELETE FROM edges WHERE source_scanner = ? runs BEFORE BulkUpsertEdges for each linker. This prevents phantom relationships from accumulating across re-scans while preserving: (1) scan-phase edges (source_scanner is NULL), (2) manually-created edges (source_scanner is NULL), (3) edges from other linker scanners. Each linker only cleans up its own edges.

When adding a parameter to a widely-called function (RunScanner gained knownNodeIDs), use a nilable type (map[string]bool) so existing callers can pass nil for backward-compatible behavior. All 9 call sites updated: 7 pass nil (scan-phase), 2 pass populated map (link-phase). The ValidateOutput function already accepted this param — the change just threaded it through RunScanner.

BulkUpsertEdges FK violations are logged via log.Printf but NOT returned to the CLI caller — so they don't appear in scanResult.Warnings. The user sees edge count but can't tell which edges were skipped. Future improvement: return violations as structured data alongside the count.

ts-morph's in-memory file system (useInMemoryFileSystem: true with createSourceFile) is perfect for testing import chain tracing — you can create complete multi-file import graphs without touching disk. 20 tests run in <1s.
DECISION: Separated matcher.ts (Prisma detection) from tracer.ts (import walking). Tracer delegates to matcher for entity discovery at each file. This keeps concerns clean and allows matcher to be independently testable.
FACT: findPrismaAccesses returns entity node IDs (e.g., entity:User), not entity names. The tracer's file-level cache stores IDs directly. Initial agent implementation had a cache consistency bug where cache stored names but lookup expected IDs.
PATTERN: When ts-morph Project has no tsconfig, use numeric enum values for ModuleKind (99=ESNext), ModuleResolutionKind (100=Node16), ScriptTarget (99=ESNext) to avoid importing compiler-internal enum types.

TS linker scanner architecture — 4 modules in pipeline: index.ts (stdin/stdout IO, builds routeNodes/entityByName maps from ExistingNodes) → parser.ts (ts-morph Project setup, route iteration, edge dedup via Set) → tracer.ts (depth-limited DFS through imports, visited-set cycle protection, file-level entity cache) → matcher.ts (PrismaClient variable resolution via 3 heuristics, PropertyAccessExpression chain walking for prisma.model.method() detection). Linker emits only edges (nodes: never[]), edge kind: touches_entity, edge ID: edge:\${routeId}-touches_entity-\${entityId}, tracePath in properties for debugging.

oRPC handler resolution is fundamentally harder than Express. Express routes have handler metadata co-located in the route's source file. oRPC contracts and implementations live in different files — the linker must reverse-search for files that import the contract and call .handler()/.func(). Current unit tests only cover direct Prisma access and import chains. Integration test (aba-iu1.6) must validate oRPC routes specifically.

touches_entity edge format — ID: edge:\${routeNodeId}-touches_entity-\${entityNodeId}, srcId: routeNodeId, dstId: entityNodeId, kind: touches_entity, properties: { tracePath: string[] }. Deduplication required: same route→entity pair via multiple import paths should emit one edge. tracePath records first-discovered path (DFS order, not necessarily shortest).

When delegating code to Sonnet agents, explicitly specify the data type at every interface boundary. Don't say 'cache the entities' — say 'cache the entity node IDs (e.g., entity:User), not the entity names (e.g., user)'. The cache consistency bug (names vs IDs) was caught in review but would have been prevented by more precise prompts.

Critical cache bug in tracer.ts — fileEntityCache stores only direct findPrismaAccesses results, not transitive entities from imports. When cache hit occurs (line 54-61), it returns early without tracing imports. First route gets full DFS, subsequent routes get only direct-entity cache hits (usually 0 for service files). Fix: traceFile must return full transitive entity set and cache that.

Depth-poisoned cache — fileEntityCache is depth-unaware. A file visited at depth 7 (remaining=1) caches a tiny entity set. When later visited at depth 0 (remaining=8), the cache returns the tiny set. Fix: cache entries must store the remaining depth at which they were explored. Only use cache when currentRemaining <= cachedRemaining.

# Log final knowledge and close the integration test bead
FACT: Hourly project integration test results — 1001 nodes (750 routes, 179 entities, 72 pages), 19253 edges (247 field_relation from Prisma, 19006 touches_entity from linker-ts). All 180 Express src routes linked. 390 oRPC routes unlinked (contract sourceFile issue). Performance: 13.8s total pipeline, 0 errors. Two follow-up beads created: aba-l55 (oRPC handler resolution), aba-c7z (barrel file noise).

Two cache bugs found and fixed in tracer.ts: (1) Cache stored only direct entities, not transitive — fixed by making traceFile return Set<string> and caching full transitive set. (2) Cache was depth-unaware — a file visited at depth 7 cached a shallow result that poisoned later visits at depth 0. Fixed by storing remainingDepth with each cache entry and only serving cache hits when currentRemaining <= cachedRemaining.

bd close aba-iu1.6
bead:
# Log final knowledge and close the integration test bead
aba-iu1.6

aba-iu1.6

bd close aba-iu1.6

content:
cd /Users/mjn/workplace/abacus

# Entry 1: Root cause investigation
Two interacting cache bugs caused the linker to link only 1/180 routes (358 edges instead of 19,253). Bug 1: fileEntityCache stored only direct findPrismaAccesses results, not transitive entities from imports — traceFile was void so child entities never propagated upward for caching. Bug 2: cache was depth-unaware — a file visited at depth 7 (remaining=1) cached a tiny entity set that poisoned later visits at depth 0 (remaining=8). The bugs compounded: fix 1 alone gave 10/180 routes because depth-poisoning still limited most routes. Both fixes together gave 180/180 routes linked.

# Entry 2: Core pattern
PATTERN: Depth-aware transitive DFS cache — when caching results of a depth-limited recursive traversal shared across multiple entry points: (1) the recursive function must RETURN its full transitive result set (direct + child results), not void; (2) cache entries must store {entities, remainingDepth} not just entities; (3) cache hits only valid when currentRemaining <= cachedRemaining; (4) overwrites are safe because deeper exploration is always a superset. Without (1), cache stores partial results. Without (2-3), shallow-first visits poison deeper-later visits.

# Entry 3: Diagnostic pattern
PATTERN: DFS cache bug detection — symptoms: (a) order-dependent results (reordering entry points changes output), (b) first-one-works-rest-fail (first traversal correct, subsequent get cached wrong results), (c) shallow results for deep chains. Invariants to assert: transitivity (cached set for N superset of union of children's cached sets), monotonicity (higher depth budget => superset of lower budget result), idempotency (clearing cache doesn't change result). Test strategy: diamond dependency graph with two entry points sharing an intermediate file.

# Entry 4: Update the stale architecture pattern
PATTERN: [SUPERSEDES earlier file-level cache pattern] Corrected tracer cache design: Map<filePath, {entities: Set<string>, remainingDepth: number}>.
